topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, a bright, unwelcome intrusion on the otherwise quiet afternoon. My phone screen lit up with a profile picture I instantly recognized: Mr. Harrison, my notoriously strict history teacher. My stomach clenched, a cold knot tightening beneath my ribs. I cautiously tapped the notification, and there he was—a recent post about a book club meeting, a picture of him smiling warmly, captioned with an encouraging quote. A slow, creeping dread began to pool in my chest. How? I hadn't interacted with him online in years. I quickly minimized the app, my fingers trembling slightly as I tried to steady my breathing. I glanced toward the classroom door, half-expecting him to materialize, standing there, watching me. The air in the room suddenly felt thick, suffocating.

[story 2]

I was scrolling through my feed, mindlessly swiping past mundane updates, when a familiar name flashed across the screen. It was Ms. Albright, my English instructor. My heart gave a sudden, erratic thump against my ribs. I stopped breathing for a second, my thumb hovering over the profile picture. It was her—the same serene, slightly amused expression she wore in class. Then I saw it: a story update, a short video clip of her laughing while holding a coffee mug. A wave of intense unease washed over me, making my palms instantly slick. I snatched the phone away as if burned, my breath catching in my throat. I stared at the blank screen, my gaze darting around the room as if searching for an escape route, feeling a profound sense of exposure, as if every secret I’d ever kept was now laid bare.

[story 3]

It was late, and I was finally relaxing on my bed, scrolling through old messages when I saw it. A notification from Mr. Davies, my calculus professor. He had posted a photo from a recent conference, looking relaxed and approachable. My hands began to shake uncontrollably. I felt a sudden, intense pressure building behind my eyes, a sensation that made focusing on anything else impossible. I pressed my forehead against the cool glass of my laptop screen, trying to physically distance myself from the display. Every muscle in my body seemed to tense up; I could feel the blood draining from my face. The silence of my room amplified the frantic pounding in my ears, a desperate need to check if anyone else had seen this, or if this was somehow a trap.
